2023 · ICLR 2024 · Agents

GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

466 hand-crafted questions that 92% of human annotators answer correctly but that the best assistant configuration tested — GPT-4 with hand-picked plugins — answers only about 15% of the time overall, and exactly 0% of the time on the hardest tier. The trick: make the questions trivial for people and brutal for machines, so the benchmark stays meaningful as models improve instead of getting quietly solved by better pattern-matching.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Grégoire Mialon, Clémentine Fourrier, Craig Swift, Thomas Wolf, Yann LeCun, Thomas Scialom · Meta FAIR / Hugging Face / AutoGPT
venue ICLR 2024 citations 998 topic Agents source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2023, the field had settled into a predictable rhythm: publish a benchmark, watch models saturate it within a year or two, publish a harder one. GLUE gave way to SuperGLUE; both fell within a couple of years. MMLU — 15,000+ questions spanning 57 subjects from STEM to law — was supposed to probe professional-level knowledge, and GPT-4 was already at 86.4% on it, comfortably above the 34.5% non-specialist human baseline and closing in on the 89.8% estimated for actual domain experts. The pattern repeats with GSM8K-style math benchmarks. The field's implicit strategy for "harder" was harder for humans — bar exams, olympiad problems, graduate coursework — and that strategy has a structural flaw: tasks that are difficult for humans are not necessarily difficult for a large model trained on most of the internet, especially once test-adjacent content leaks into pretraining data. A benchmark built around human difficulty decays exactly when it succeeds at being hard, because success looks identical to contamination.

The second half of the problem is evaluation itself. As soon as a benchmark demands open-ended generation rather than multiple choice — writing a coherent essay, solving a proof, executing a multi-step task — grading it stops being free. Human evaluation doesn't scale and gets slower exactly as task complexity rises (how do you have a non-expert grade a generated proof, or an AI-written book?). Model-based evaluation swaps that bottleneck for a different one: it requires a judge model that is itself trustworthy, is usually a dependency on a stronger, more expensive model than the one being tested, and carries known biases — for instance a documented tendency to prefer whichever answer is presented first. Aggregated holistic benchmarks (HELM-style compilations of many datasets) sidestep some of this but introduce their own problem: they can be hard to use efficiently or reliably at scale, and multiple-choice formats make contamination assessment specifically harder, since a model can land on the correct letter through a wrong or memorized reasoning path with no way to tell the difference from the final score alone.

GAIA's authors — a Meta FAIR / Hugging Face / AutoGPT collaboration — also survey the existing crop of "general assistant" evaluations and find each one falls short in a different way. ToolQA and Gentopia repurpose existing QA datasets (MMLU, MATH) with human annotations, which risks contamination during training and doesn't guarantee tool use actually happened rather than being bypassed. Gorilla's APIBench and API-Bank test whether a model calls the right API syntax, which measures API-fluency rather than open-world task completion. AgentBench is broader, but its closed-box environments (from Unix shells to WebShopping APIs) risk rewarding models for having learned the quirks of a specific sandboxed API rather than for general competence at operating in the real, messy world. OpenAGI is the closest relative — a platform plus benchmark of multi-step, multi-modal tasks — but its tasks are anchored to current model capabilities rather than to a fixed target that stays meaningful as capabilities advance. GAIA's proposal is to flip the difficulty axis entirely: keep the questions conceptually simple — even tedious — for a human, but require an AI system to autonomously plan and execute a real sequence of actions (browsing, reading files, running code, combining multiple modalities) to reach a single, unambiguous, hard-to-fake answer. It is explicitly framed as a Proof-of-Work analogue: expensive to solve honestly, cheap to verify.

// how it works

The method

GAIA is 466 questions, each with a single correct, unambiguous, and usually short answer — a number, a short string, or a comma-separated list of either. Some questions arrive with an attached file: an image, a spreadsheet, a PDF, an audio clip. Answering one typically means locating and combining information from several different sources — a web page here, an attached file there — and producing a precise value, not writing an essay. The three examples the authors lead with make the shape of the task concrete: a Level 1 question just requires finding a specific enrollment count on a clinical-trials web page; a Level 2 question needs a photographed nutrition label combined with a remembered/looked-up regulatory standard to compute a percentage; a Level 3 question chains an old NASA "Astronomy Picture of the Day" caption, group membership among named astronauts, and up-to-date biographical data to isolate a single number.

Four design principles

The benchmark is built around four explicit commitments. Real-world and challenging for AI, not for humans — the questions draw on quick adaptation via reasoning, multi-modal understanding, and diverse tool use rather than specialized domain knowledge, deliberately inverting the harder-for-humans trend. Interpretability — because the human pass rate is near-perfect and the question set is small and hand-curated rather than aggregated from thousands of auto-generated items, a reader can actually inspect a model's reasoning trace and judge whether it did the right thing, in contrast to benchmarks whose scale makes that kind of qualitative auditing impractical. Non-gameability — solving a question requires successfully executing some number of steps across a genuinely large action space; because the final answer is engineered to not already exist in plain text anywhere on the web, a system cannot simply memorize its way to the right response the way it might on a multiple-choice test, where a wrong reasoning trace can still land on the correct letter by chance. Simplicity of use — because every ground truth is factoid, concise, and unambiguous, scoring is a deterministic, zero-shot, quasi-exact-match comparison, which sidesteps the sensitivity many LLM benchmarks have to prompt wording, few-shot exemplar choice, or harness implementation details.

Accuracy = Σᵢ 1[ normalize(âᵢ) = normalize(aᵢ*) ]N // type-aware normalization: numbers drop commas/units unless asked for, strings drop articles and abbreviations and spell out digits, lists are comma-separated per-element — no partial credit, no judge model, single correct answer per question

Three levels, defined by steps and tools

Questions are grouped into three difficulty tiers, operationalized by the number of steps and distinct tools the annotators themselves needed while crafting and answering each question — an imperfect but explicit proxy, since there is no single canonical path to most answers. Level 1 generally needs no tool, or at most one, and no more than 5 steps (146 questions). Level 2 generally needs multiple different tools combined across roughly 5–10 steps (245 questions). Level 3 is reserved for a "near-perfect general assistant": arbitrarily long action sequences, any number of tools, and effectively unrestricted access to the world (75 questions). The authors are explicit that the boundaries are fuzzy — a question with fewer than 10 annotator steps but genuinely gnarly web navigation might still get bumped to Level 3.

Some GAIA questions come with an attached file, and the distribution skews toward exactly the messy, everyday formats a real assistant has to cope with: spreadsheets (29 xlsx files) are the single largest category, followed by images (18 png, 7 jpg), PDFs (15), plain text (13), and audio (7 mp3) — with a long tail of docx, pptx, csv, zip, xml, json, python scripts, and even a protein-structure .pdb file and a video .mov, one apiece. None of this is exotic file-format research; it's the ordinary clutter that "read this attachment and tell me the answer" entails in practice.

Crafting and validating questions

The authors wrote seed questions themselves, then handed them — with a written rubric — to compensated annotators (a collaboration with Surge AI) to write more. The instructions are concrete and worth reproducing in spirit: ground every question in a cited "source of truth" (Wikipedia, arXiv, GitHub, or an attached document, and combine multiple sources for the harder tiers); make sure the answer doesn't already exist in plain text online; keep answers to a number or a few words; make sure the answer won't drift over time (accounting for possible deletion of the source); and — added after the fact — check the source page's robots.txt so that an AI assistant is actually permitted to fetch it. Every crafted question is annotated with its own steps, tools used, and time taken, which is the raw material that later becomes the level label.

Validation is a second, independent pass: two fresh annotators answer each newly crafted question blind, with no knowledge of the intended answer. Across 623 newly crafted questions (1,246 independent re-answers), the two new annotators agreed with the original answer 55% of the time, one agreed while the other disagreed 27% of the time, and both disagreed 18% of the time. A question only counts as "valid" if it survives this cross-check — 68% did on aggregate, but that number falls sharply with difficulty: 75% of Level 1 questions were valid as-written, versus 68% at Level 2 and just 47% at Level 3. Everything that failed validation was either repaired or discarded. Building and validating a single question, including repairs, is estimated at roughly two hours of annotator time. The resulting human pass rate — 92% aggregate, 94% / 92% / 87% by level — is what the paper reports as the human baseline for GAIA.

Evaluating a model

Because every ground truth is a short factoid string, evaluating a system needs nothing beyond API access: a fixed system prompt instructs the model to end its response with FINAL ANSWER: [answer], spells out formatting rules (no thousands-commas or unit symbols in numbers unless asked for, no articles or abbreviations in strings, digits spelled out), and the response is scored by a quasi-exact string match against the ground truth after type-aware normalization. There's no rubric, no judge model, and no held-out reasoning-trace grading — deliberately, in service of the "simplicity of use" principle.

166 of the 466 questions are released with full annotations (steps, tools, answer) as a "developer set" for building and sanity-checking a pipeline; the remaining 300 were held back, answers withheld, to power a hidden leaderboard (later hosted on Hugging Face, per the paper's release notes).

// what they found

The results

The headline gap is stark: human annotators answer 92% of GAIA questions correctly in aggregate, while the strongest configuration tested — GPT-4 augmented with plugins — answers roughly 15% correctly across the full question set. Broken out by level, no automated method of any kind reaches above 30.3%, and every single one — including that best "oracle" configuration — scores exactly 0% on all 75 Level 3 questions.

MethodLevel 1 (n=146)Level 2 (n=245)Level 3 (n=75)
Search engine (no LLM, human types query)7.4%0%0%
GPT-4 (no tools)9.1 ± 2.5%2.6 ± 0.6%0%
GPT-4 Turbo (no tools)13.0 ± 2.1%5.5 ± 1.4%0%
AutoGPT (GPT-4 backend)14.4%0.4%0%
GPT-4 + plugins ("oracle")30.3%9.7%0%
Human annotator93.9%91.8%87.3%

Time-to-answer adds a second axis that the accuracy numbers alone hide. Human annotators average 6.8 minutes on Level 1, 10.5 on Level 2, and 17.7 on Level 3 — the paper rounds this to "6 to 17 minutes." GPT-4 without tools answers almost instantly (roughly 0.15–0.24 minutes) but is usually wrong. AutoGPT, notably, is neither fast nor accurate: it averages about 7.6 minutes per question at Level 1 and 11.7 minutes at Level 2 — comparable to or slower than a human — while scoring far below even bare GPT-4 at Level 2 (0.4% vs. 2.6%). The authors single out the human-plus-GPT-4-plugins combination as offering the best score-per-time ratio observed, precisely because a human is doing the tool selection that AutoGPT tries and largely fails to automate.

Splitting Level 1 performance by required capability (web browsing, coding, multi-modality, diverse filetype reading, or none) shows exactly where tool access matters and where it doesn't. Bare GPT-4 is essentially unable to touch the "diverse filetype reading" or "multi-modality" buckets — its non-zero scores there mostly come from questions that happen to be solvable a different way than the annotators solved them, not from actually reading the file. Adding plugins narrows the gap in every bucket but does not close it, and even the best configuration never exceeds roughly 20 percentage points of raw score contribution in any single capability at Level 1. Non-zero web-browsing scores for the non-augmented models are attributed mostly to memorized facts standing in for an actual lookup, since those models cannot browse at all.

The search-engine control is a useful sanity check on its own: a human typing the question straight into a search box and skimming the first page of results gets the right answer 7.4% of the time at Level 1 and 0% at Level 2 and 3. That rules out the concern that GAIA questions are just barely-disguised search queries, and shows that an LLM assistant — even a mediocre one — is doing something qualitatively different from, and better than, plain search on this task distribution, even though the best assistant tested is still nowhere near human level.

Qualitatively, the paper's worked examples (Figures 9–11 in the appendix) illustrate specific failure and success modes. GPT-4 without browsing simply refuses questions requiring information past its knowledge cutoff ("I'm unable to browse the internet... FINAL ANSWER: Unable to provide"), while the browsing-enabled version resolves the identical question in a single well-targeted search. The GPT-4 browsing plugin also shows genuine multi-step behavior — refining an initial query ("Goldfinger ending scene object color") into a more specific one ("Goldfinger ending scene parachute color") when the first search doesn't land the answer. But tool access is not the whole story: GPT-4 also fails a self-contained Rubik's-cube deduction puzzle that needs no tools at all and is labeled Level 1, producing "Red, Yellow" against a ground truth of "green, white" — a reminder that some of GAIA's difficulty is plain multi-step logical bookkeeping, independent of browsing or file-reading.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

GAIA's central idea — build questions that are cheap to verify and hard to fake, and hold the difficulty bar fixed instead of chasing harder-for-humans content — is a genuinely useful correction to a benchmark culture that kept confusing "harder for people" with "harder for models." The 92%-vs-~15% gap it exposed in November 2023 was a real and legible signal about how far tool-using assistants were from general competence. But several parts of the design deserve a skeptical second look, and the paper is admirably candid about most of them itself.

Start with the headline number itself: the strongest score in the paper, GPT-4 + plugins at 30.3% / 9.7% / 0%, is explicitly labeled by the authors as an "oracle" result. The plugins used for a given question were hand-selected by the researchers, not chosen autonomously by any agent — and the paper states outright that this configuration "cannot be reproduced exactly," because ChatGPT's plugin ecosystem at the time changed and disappeared unpredictably, and the official search plugin was pulled and later restored mid-experiment. Meanwhile the one baseline that does select tools autonomously, AutoGPT, is both slower than a human and worse than plain GPT-4 at Level 2. So the paper's best number is a human-curated ceiling on GPT-4's raw capability, not a demonstration that any actual autonomous system reaches 15% — a distinction the abstract's single "92% vs. 15%" framing can obscure if a reader doesn't dig into Table 4.

The benchmark is also now over two years old, and it was built against a very specific November-2023 stack: bare GPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo, manually-selected ChatGPT plugins, and 2023-era AutoGPT. Every method the paper tests scores exactly 0% at Level 3 — but that floor was measured before frontier reasoning models, native multimodal models, and dedicated agentic tool-use loops existed as products. A 0% floor from a specific, now-dated agent stack is a much weaker claim than a 0% floor that would hold against 2026's best "deep research" agents; the paper's own framing (a system that solves GAIA would sit "one level below" a competent general AI, per the contemporaneous Morris et al. "Levels of AGI" framework) was explicitly a bet about the near future, not a permanent verdict.

On contamination, the authors' stated mitigation is that no ground-truth answer exists in plain text anywhere on the web — verified in part by the near-zero search-engine baseline — which blocks naive memorization of the final answer string. It does not, however, block memorization or leakage of the constituent facts a question asks a model to combine, and it does not address a contamination vector specific to browsing benchmarks: GAIA's own 166-question developer set (published with full annotated answers), its Hugging Face leaderboard, and the growing pile of blog posts and papers discussing specific GAIA questions are themselves now crawlable web pages. A browsing-enabled agent evaluated on GAIA today can, in principle, search its way to a page discussing the exact question and its answer — a risk the paper's general discussion of benchmark "decay" and "catastrophic contamination" names in the abstract but doesn't work through for this specific self-referential case.

The headline metric is also, by design, blind to everything except the final answer string. Section 6 of the paper names this directly: GAIA "does not evaluate the trace leading to the answer," and there's no simple way to grade the many different valid paths to a correct number. Practically, that means a system that stumbles onto the right answer through a lucky or unsound shortcut scores identically to one that reasoned soundly; unsafe or wasteful intermediate behavior (spamming a site, ignoring robots.txt despite the authors' own guideline, hallucinating an intermediate fact that happens to cancel out) is invisible to the score; and there's no partial credit, so a system 90% of the way through a genuinely hard Level 3 chain scores the same 0 as one that never engaged with the question — defensible under the paper's "full automation" framing, but a coarse instrument for anyone trying to see where, specifically, a modern agent's multi-step reasoning breaks down.

Finally, the human baseline that anchors the whole comparison is softer than "92% human accuracy" suggests on its face. It comes from the same pool of compensated Surge AI annotators who wrote the questions, doing an independent validation pass — not a separately recruited, blinded panel. The paper's own validation statistics show real disagreement even within that pool: only 55% of freshly written questions got unanimous agreement from two new annotators, and validity fell to 47% at Level 3 before repair or removal. The reported 87.3% human score at Level 3 is measured on the subset that survived that filtering and repair loop, not on Level-3 question-writing attempts in general — a reasonable methodological choice, but one that means the human ceiling and the benchmark's construction process are more entangled than a single clean number implies. The Datacard is also candid that GAIA is English-only, drawn mostly from English web pages, authored by a demographically narrow annotator pool (57% male / 43% female, 61% with only a Bachelor's degree, all US-based), and by the authors' own admission "will therefore not validate the usefulness of assistants for non-English speakers (80% of the global population)... nor on the non-English-speaking web (about half of its content)."

baselines

The best score (GPT-4 + plugins) is an explicitly-labeled, non-reproducible "oracle" with human-picked tools; the one genuinely autonomous baseline (AutoGPT) is both slower than a human and worse than bare GPT-4 at Level 2.

contamination

Final answers are kept out of plain-text web pages by design, but the benchmark's own dev set, leaderboard, and secondary discussion are now crawlable — a leakage vector specific to browsing agents that the paper doesn't examine in depth.

what's missed

Only the final answer string is graded — no partial credit, no reasoning-trace check, no visibility into unsafe or wasteful intermediate steps; the authors name this as GAIA's biggest open gap themselves.

would raise confidence

A reproducible fully-autonomous baseline near the oracle's level; coarse grading of intermediate steps; periodic re-validation against contamination from the benchmark's own growing web footprint; and a broader, non-English annotator pool.

// bottom line

GAIA's reframing — trivial for humans, hard-to-fake for machines, cheap to grade — is a real methodological contribution, and the gap it measured (92% vs. roughly 15%, 0% at Level 3) was a genuine and influential signal in late 2023. Read the headline number as "a human-curated ceiling on a specific, now-dated agent stack," not as a durable measure of autonomous agent capability, and treat the final-answer-only scoring as exactly what the authors call it: simple and unambiguous, but blind to how — and how safely — a system got there.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. GAIA inverts the usual "make it harder for humans" benchmark strategy: its 466 questions are conceptually easy for people (92% aggregate human accuracy) but demand genuine multi-step, multi-tool execution from an AI — closer to a Proof-of-Work design than an exam, and scored by quasi-exact string match rather than a judge model or human raters.
  2. The strongest reported score, GPT-4 + plugins at 30.3% / 9.7% / 0% by level, is an explicitly labeled non-reproducible "oracle" built on hand-picked plugins; the genuinely autonomous baseline, AutoGPT, is slower than a human and worse than bare GPT-4 at Level 2 — and every method tested, oracle included, scores exactly 0% on the hardest 75-question tier.
  3. Grading only the final answer keeps evaluation cheap and unambiguous, but the authors themselves flag it as the benchmark's biggest limitation: no partial credit, no reasoning-trace check, and — as GAIA's own dev set and leaderboard become part of the crawlable web — a growing contamination surface specific to browsing agents that a static answer-matching score can't detect.

Grégoire Mialon, Clémentine Fourrier, Craig Swift, Thomas Wolf, Yann LeCun, Thomas Scialom · 2023 · ICLR 2024
